explanation: Pratyaksa is direct, immediate cognition.
Nirvikalpaka perception is indeterminate, without conceptual classification.
Savikalpaka perception includes conceptual determination.
Extraordinary perception includes forms such as Samanyalaksana.
Sense-object contact is central to ordinary perceptual cognition.
Illusion is invalid cognition, i.e., Aprama.
Perception depends on functioning sense faculties.
Yogaja perception is classified as extraordinary perception.
Pratyabhijna means recognition.
Anumana depends on vyapti, the invariable relation.
A valid hetu must support a universal relation to the sadhya.
Paksha is the subject under consideration in inference.
Classic inference: smoke indicates fire under known vyapti.
Vyapti is an invariable universal concomitance.
Hetvabhasa is the fallacious reason in Nyaya logic.
Nyaya syllogism has five members.
Drstanta is the familiar example in inference.
Nigamana is the concluding member of syllogism.
Three common types are Purvavat, Sesavat, and Samanyato drsta.
Upamana is knowledge through comparison/similarity.
Gavaya example is standard for Upamana.
Upamana relies on perceived similarity relation.
Nyaya treats Upamana as an independent pramana.
Shabda depends on trustworthy authority (apta-vakya).
Apta denotes a reliable, competent speaker.
Classical view treats Vedic testimony as authorless.
Shabda can provide knowledge beyond immediate perception.
Shabda is verbal testimony-based knowledge.
Arthapatti is postulation/presumption to resolve inconsistency.
The classic fat-man-fasting case illustrates Arthapatti.
Mimamsa explicitly accepts Arthapatti as pramana.
Arthapatti is used to resolve apparent contradiction.
Arthapatti yields mediated/indirect cognition.
Anupalabdhi is cognition of absence/non-apprehension.
Bhatta Mimamsa accepts Anupalabdhi as pramana.
No-pot-on-floor is a classic absence-cognition case.
Knowledge of absence is attributed to Anupalabdhi in relevant schools.
Anupalabdhi concerns negative cognition.
Nyaya accepts four pramanas.
Vaisesika traditionally accepts two pramanas.
Nyaya accepts only four pramanas: Pratyaksa, Anumana, Upamana, Sabda. Arthapatti is NOT among them.
Classical Nyaya school accepts exactly four pramanas: Pratyaksa, Anumana, Upamana, Sabda.
Arthapatti is specifically accepted as an independent pramana by Mimamsa and Advaita Vedanta.
Nyaya defines perception (pratyaksa) as knowledge produced by sense-object contact (indriyartha-sannikarsa).
Nyaya lists three types: Purvavat, Sesavat, Samanyato drsta. Kevalanyogin is NOT a standard Nyaya type.
Carvaka (Lokayata) accepts only Pratyaksa (direct perception), rejecting inference, comparison and testimony.
In Nyaya, anumana is grounded in vyapti - the invariable universal concomitance between hetu and sadhya.
The fat man who does not eat by day must eat by night - this is the classic illustration of Arthapatti (postulation).
Abhava (Anupalabdhi) is the pramana that yields knowledge of non-existence or absence of an object.
Buddhist epistemologists (Dignaga, Dharmakirti) accept only two pramanas: Pratyaksa and Anumana.
Sabda pramana is knowledge from authoritative verbal testimony - the source of linguistic and scriptural understanding.
Nyaya explicitly accepts Nirvikalpaka (indeterminate) perception as the first stage of perceptual cognition.

keywords: who does not accept upamana as independent
explanation: Buddhist epistemologists reject Upamana as an independent pramana, reducing it to Anumana (inference from similarity).
Sesavat anumana infers the cause from the observed effect - e.g., inferring past rain from a swollen river.
Samanyato drsta anumana is based on general class observation of invariable concomitance without a direct causal relation.
Pratyaksa (direct perception) is the primary pramana for empirical sciences in Indian philosophy.
The defining characteristic of prama is yatharthata - the cognition must faithfully conform to its object as it actually is.
Pramata is the knowing subject - the agent who exercises a pramana to obtain valid cognition (prama).
Carvaka holds that anumana is ultimately dependent on and reducible to pratyaksa - inference not grounded in perception is unreliable.
A valid hetu in Nyaya must satisfy five characteristics: Paksasat, Sapaksasat, Vipaksasat, Badhita, Asatpratipaksa.
Vyapti (invariable concomitance) is the ground of valid inference, NOT a fallacy. The five Hetvabhasas are Savyabhicara, Viruddha, Satpratipaksa, Asiddha, Badhita.
Savyabhicara is the fallacy of an irregular hetu present in both sapaksa and vipaksa, making the reason non-conclusive.
Critics (especially Buddhist logicians) argue that Upamana is merely a form of Anumana and not an independent pramana.
In Advaita Vedanta, Brahman (nirguna) transcends perception and inference; it is known only through Sabda - the Upanishads.
Vaisesika traditionally accepts only two pramanas: Pratyaksa and Anumana, rejecting Upamana and Sabda as independent.
Viparyaya is erroneous cognition - a form of Aprama where the object is apprehended contrary to its actual nature (e.g. rope-snake error).

Pratyaksha (perception) is the foundational pramana that ultimately verifies all knowledge. Inference and testimony must ultimately trace back to perceptual grounding — without perception, the vyapti itself cannot be established.

The classic Nyaya smoke-fire inference: the hill has fire because it has smoke. Wherever there is smoke there is fire (vyapti). Smoke on hill (hetu) + vyapti = fire on hill (sadhya). This is the canonical example of Anumana.

The Nyaya panchavayava has five members: Pratijna (proposition), Hetu (reason), Udaharana (example with general rule), Upanaya (application to the case), Nigamana (conclusion). This distinguishes Nyaya inference from Western syllogism.

Anupalabdhi is knowledge of the absence of an object derived from its non-perception in a place where it would be perceptible if it existed. Example: there is no jar on the table because I don't perceive it there.
Classic Anupalabdhi example: there is no jar on the table because I do not perceive it there — the absence is known through non-perception.
Absence (abhava) is known through Anupalabdhi — non-perception of an object that would be perceptible if present.

answer_text: Anupalabdhi requires perceptual conditions but infers absence, not presence
explanation: Anupalabdhi and Pratyaksha both require perceptual conditions. But Pratyaksha gives direct positive knowledge of presence; Anupalabdhi gives knowledge of absence from the very failure of perception.

Chala is a debate fault where the respondent misrepresents the speaker's meaning by deliberately exploiting ambiguity — substituting a secondary or irrelevant meaning of a word for the intended primary one.
Chala (quibble) is the Nyaya debate fault of exploiting a word's ambiguity to misrepresent the opponent's argument.
Jati is a debate fault where the respondent raises irrelevant or false objections by drawing superficial analogies that do not address the actual argument. There are 24 types of Jati in Nyaya.
Jati (futile rejoinder) is the debate fault of raising false objections through superficial analogies. Nyaya recognizes 24 types of Jati.

Nigrahasthana is the occasion when a debater is legitimately defeated — through misunderstanding the opponent's argument, self-contradiction, silence, or repetition. Nyaya lists 22 grounds for defeat.
Nigrahasthana (point of defeat) is when a debater has committed a debating error that legitimately ends their argument. 22 grounds are recognized in Nyaya.
Vitanda is a debate mode where the goal is only to refute the opponent without establishing any thesis of one's own. Unlike Vada (truth-seeking) and Jalpa (victory-seeking), Vitanda only tears down.
Vitanda is the Nyaya debate mode of pure destructive argument — refuting without establishing any positive thesis. Considered the lowest form of discourse.
Vada is the highest form of debate in Nyaya — a truth-seeking discussion where both parties use valid means of knowledge, accept valid conclusions, and aim for truth rather than victory.
Jalpa is debate aimed at winning rather than truth — using any means including quibbles (chala) and futile rejoinders (jati) to defeat the opponent.
Vada is the proper, truth-seeking form of debate in Nyaya. It uses valid arguments and aims to establish truth, not merely win.
Jalpa uses any means including chala (quibble) and jati (futile rejoinder) to defeat an opponent without necessarily seeking truth.
Nyaya recognizes three forms of debate: Vada (honest/truth-seeking), Jalpa (wrangling/victory-seeking), and Vitanda (pure destructive refutation). Only Vada leads to valid knowledge.

Savyabhicara is the hetvabhasa where the hetu (reason) is undistributed — it appears in both sapaksa (positive examples) and vipaksa (negative examples). Example: 'sound is eternal because it is knowable' — knowable applies to both eternal and non-eternal things.

Viruddha is the hetvabhasa where the reason (hetu) actually proves the opposite of what is intended. Example: 'sound is eternal because it is produced' — but 'produced things are impermanent', so the hetu contradicts the sadhya.
Satpratipaksha is the hetvabhasa where an equally valid counter-reason exists that establishes the opposite conclusion. When two equally supported inferences conflict, both are suspended.

Asiddha is the hetvabhasa where the hetu (reason) itself is not established or proven. There are three types: Svarupasiddha (reason non-existent in subject), Ashrayasiddha (subject non-existent), Vyapyatvasiddha (reason's scope unproven).

Badhita is the hetvabhasa where the sadhya (conclusion) is contradicted by a stronger pramana (usually perception). Example: inferring fire is cold from smoke, when perception directly shows fire is hot.

Nyaya recognizes five hetvabhasa (fallacious reasons): Savyabhicara (erratic), Viruddha (contradictory), Satpratipaksha (counterbalanced), Asiddha (unestablished), and Badhita (refuted by perception).

Hetvabhasa (semblant reason) is the generic term for all types of fallacious reasoning in Nyaya inference. A hetu that appears to be a valid reason but actually violates one of the conditions for valid vyapti.

Upamana (comparison) is knowledge of a relation between a name and a named object through similarity. One who has been told that a gavaya resembles a cow recognizes a gavaya when seen in the forest by comparing it with the known cow.

The canonical Upamana example: a person told 'a gavaya resembles a cow' goes to a forest and recognizes a gavaya. The recognition is Upamana — knowledge through similarity/comparison, not direct perception or inference.
Upamana (comparison) is the pramana that produces knowledge through similarity — recognizing a new object by comparing it with something previously known.
Upamana requires prior verbal instruction (a form of Shabda) establishing the similarity. Without being told 'a gavaya resembles a cow', the recognition cannot occur as Upamana.

Classic Arthapatti example: Devadatta is fat but does not eat during the day. To explain this contradiction, we must postulate that he eats at night. Arthapatti provides the only coherent explanation for an otherwise inexplicable observation.

Arthapatti (postulation/presumption) is the knowledge of an unperceived fact assumed to explain a perceived fact that cannot be explained otherwise. It involves inferring the necessary precondition of an observed anomaly.
Arthapatti (postulation) is the pramana that assumes a fact not otherwise known in order to explain an observed fact. It is distinct from anumana because it does not use a pre-established vyapti.
Prameya are the 12 objects of valid knowledge in Nyaya: self, body, senses, sense-objects, cognition, mind, activity, defects, rebirth, result, suffering, and liberation.
The ultimate goal in Nyaya is Moksha — complete and final cessation of all pain and suffering. This is achieved through right knowledge (prama) of the 16 padarthas (categories).

The Nyaya Sutra was composed by Akshapada Gautama (also called Gautama). It is the foundational text of the Nyaya school of Indian philosophy, establishing the 16 padarthas and epistemological framework.
Vatsyayana wrote the first major commentary (Bhashya) on the Nyaya Sutra. His Nyayabhashya established the interpretation that dominated classical Nyaya.
Navya Nyaya (New Logic) was founded by Gangesa Upadhyaya in his Tattvacintamani. It introduced a precise technical language for epistemology and logic that influenced all later Indian philosophy.
Prama is valid cognition — yathartha (as things are) knowledge. It is produced by pramana and represents the object as it actually is. Prama is contrasted with Aprama (invalid cognition).

answer_text: Aprama (Invalid Cognition: Smriti, Samsaya, Viparyaya, Tarka)
explanation: Aprama (non-prama) includes Smriti (memory — re-presenting a past object, not a new cognition), Samsaya (doubt), Viparyaya (error/misapprehension), and sometimes Tarka (hypothetical reasoning).

Samsaya is a cognition that oscillates between two alternatives — perceiving shared characters without determining which differentiating feature belongs to the object. Example: seeing a tall figure in the distance and doubting whether it is a man or a post.

Valid vyapti requires three conditions: (1) Paksadharmata — hetu is present in the paksha (subject); (2) Sapaksasattva — hetu is present in sapaksa (positive example); (3) Vipaksavyavrtti — hetu is absent from vipaksa (negative cases).
In the smoke-fire inference, the kitchen (where there is both fire and smoke) is the canonical sapaksa — the positive example establishing that vyapti holds: wherever there is smoke, there is fire.

Kevalanvayi is an inference where the hetu and sadhya always co-exist — there is no known negative example (vipaksa). Example: 'all things are nameable because they are knowable'. Both knowability and nameability are universal.

Kevalavyatireki inference has no positive example — it is established entirely through the contrapositive: wherever the sadhya is absent, the hetu is absent. Example: 'the soul is not made of earth, for it lacks smell' (only earth has smell).
Apta-vacana is the testimony of a reliable person (apta) — one who has direct knowledge of the object, is truthful, and communicates clearly. This is the foundation for Shabda as a valid pramana.
Shabda is of two types: Vaidika (Vedic testimony — from the Vedas, considered supernaturally authoritative) and Laukika (ordinary testimony — from reliable human speakers with direct knowledge).

Nyaya philosophy recognizes 16 padarthas (categories): pramana, prameya, samsaya, prayojana, drstanta, siddhanta, avayava, tarka, nirnaya, vada, jalpa, vitanda, hetvabhasa, chala, jati, nigrahasthana.
Drstanta is a corroborative example used in inference — a case where both the hetu and the sadhya are known to co-exist (positive example). Example: the kitchen in the smoke-fire inference.
Nirnaya is the state of definitive, certain knowledge obtained after resolving doubt through inquiry. It is the goal of the discussion (vada) process — arriving at certain knowledge.

Tarka is indirect reasoning — assuming the opposite of what is to be proved and showing it leads to absurdity (reductio ad absurdum). Tarka is not an independent pramana but a supporting tool for inference.
Purvavat inference proceeds from cause to effect — based on prior observation of their connection. Example: seeing dark clouds (cause) and inferring rain (effect) based on past experience.
Sesavat inference proceeds from effect to cause — inferring a past cause from an observed effect. Example: seeing a flooded river (effect) and inferring that it rained in the mountains (cause).

Samanyato Drsta inference is based on general observation, not on a direct causal connection. Example: inferring the movement of the sun from its changing position, even though the movement itself is imperceptible.

Indian Knowledge Systems (IKS) refers to the vast corpus of traditional Indian learning including Nyaya, Vaisheshika, Samkhya, Yoga, Mimamsa, Vedanta, and many other schools. The Unriddling Inference 2026 hackathon focuses on formalizing Nyaya epistemology for AI.

The Unriddling Inference 2026 hackathon challenges participants to formalize Nyaya epistemological principles in AI systems — implementing pramana-based reasoning, hetvabhasa detection, and epistemic status classification.

Pragabhava is the prior absence of a thing before its production. Example: the absence of a pot before it is made. It has no beginning but has an end when the thing comes into existence. This is one of the four types of Abhava in Nyaya-Vaisesika.

Pradhvamsabhava is the absence of a thing after its destruction. Example: absence of a pot after it is broken. It has a beginning (the moment of destruction) but no end. One of the four types of Abhava.
Atyantabhava is the absolute or eternal absence — the object has never existed and never will. Example: the sky-flower (kha-puspa) or the horns of a rabbit. It has neither beginning nor end.
Anyonyabhava is the mutual absence or difference between two things — a pot is not a cloth, a cloth is not a pot. It expresses the mutual non-identity of distinct entities.

Nyaya-Vaisesika recognizes four types of Abhava (absence): Pragabhava (prior absence), Pradhvamsabhava (posterior absence after destruction), Atyantabhava (absolute/eternal absence), and Anyonyabhava (mutual/relational absence).
Pararthanumana is inference made public — expressed in syllogistic form for others to evaluate. It uses the five-membered Nyaya syllogism. Contrasted with Svarthanumana (inference for oneself).

Svarthanumana is private inference — the individual's own reasoning process from perceived hetu to concluded sadhya. It need not be verbalized in the five-membered form. Contrasted with Pararthanumana.

Vyapti has two forms: Anvaya-vyapti (wherever H is, S is — e.g., wherever there is smoke, there is fire) and Vyatireka-vyapti (wherever S is absent, H is absent — e.g., wherever there is no fire, there is no smoke). Both confirm valid invariable concomitance.

answer_text: Upanaya (Application of General Rule to the Case)
explanation: Upanaya is the fourth member of the Nyaya pancavayava syllogism. It applies the general rule (vyapti) from Udaharana to the present case: 'and this hill has smoke' — applying the universal rule to the specific instance.

Udaharana is the third member of the Nyaya pancavayava. It states the general rule with an example: 'Wherever there is smoke, there is fire — as in the kitchen.' It establishes the vyapti that grounds the inference.
Samkhya school accepts three pramanas: Pratyaksa (perception), Anumana (inference), and Agama (authoritative testimony/Sabda). It explicitly rejects Upamana and Arthapatti as independent pramanas.

answer_text: Six (Pratyaksa, Anumana, Upamana, Sabda, Arthapatti, Anupalabdhi)
explanation: Advaita Vedanta accepts all six pramanas: Pratyaksa, Anumana, Upamana, Sabda, Arthapatti, and Anupalabdhi. This is the most comprehensive acceptance among Indian philosophical schools.
Prabhakara Mimamsa accepts five pramanas: Pratyaksa, Anumana, Upamana, Sabda, and Arthapatti — rejecting Anupalabdhi as an independent pramana. Bhatta Mimamsa adds Anupalabdhi as the sixth.

Jain epistemology is based on Anekantavada — the doctrine that reality is many-sided (anekanta) and cannot be fully grasped from any single viewpoint. This leads to Syadvada (qualified assertion) and Nayavada (standpoint theory).
Tarkasangraha was composed by Annambhatta (17th century). It is a concise introductory text on Nyaya-Vaisesika philosophy and one of the most widely studied texts in classical Indian logic.
Nyayamanjari was written by Jayanta Bhatta (9th century CE). It is an encyclopedic commentary exploring and defending Nyaya epistemology against Buddhist and other objections.

Udayana (10th-11th century) was a pivotal Nyaya philosopher known for his Nyayakusumanjali (rational proof of God's existence) and Atmatattvaviveka. He is considered a bridge figure between Old and New (Navya) Nyaya.

Dharmakirti formalized the trirupa-linga — the three conditions a valid logical mark (linga/hetu) must satisfy: (1) present in the subject (paksadharmata), (2) present in similar cases (sapaksasattva), (3) absent from dissimilar cases (vipaksavyavrtti). This mirrors Nyaya's hetu conditions.

Gangesa Upadhyaya (13th-14th century) founded Navya Nyaya with his Tattvacintamani (Jewel of Reflection on Truth). He introduced precise technical vocabulary that revolutionized Indian epistemology and logic.

When a person who has been told 'a gavaya resembles a cow' goes to a forest and recognizes the animal they see as a gavaya — this is Upamana. The cognition arises through the similarity relation between the gavaya (new object) and the cow (known object), not through direct perception alone.

answer_text: Anumana (Sesavat type — Inference from Effect to Cause)
explanation: Inferring that it rained heavily upstream from observing a flooded river is Sesavat Anumana — inference from effect (flooded river) to cause (past rainfall). The vyapti: rivers flood when there is heavy rain upstream.

answer_text: Anumana (Purvavat type — Inference from Cause to Effect)
explanation: Inferring future rain from seeing dark clouds gathering is Purvavat Anumana — inference from cause (dark clouds) to effect (coming rain), based on prior observed connection between the two.

keywords: fat person not eating day eats night
must eat at night because fat
explanation: If Devadatta is fat but is seen to never eat during the day, we must postulate he eats at night to explain the otherwise inexplicable observation. This is Arthapatti — the only coherent explanation for a seemingly contradictory set of facts.

When I look at a table and do not see a jar — though the conditions for perception are met — I cognize the absence of the jar. This is Anupalabdhi: knowledge of absence through non-perception of what would be perceptible if it existed.

Knowledge gained from a reliable teacher (apta) or from Vedic injunctions is Shabda pramana. The validity depends on the authority (apta-vakya) of the speaker — a reliable person with direct knowledge who communicates truly.

answer_text: Viparyaya (Error / Aprama) — NOT a valid pramana
explanation: Mistaking a rope for a snake in dim light is Viparyaya — erroneous cognition (Aprama). The object (rope) is apprehended contrary to its actual nature (as snake). This is not a pramana but a failure of valid cognition, illustrating the difference between Prama and Aprama.

The inference 'sound is eternal because it is knowable' is Savyabhicara. The hetu 'knowable' is too broad — it applies to both eternal things (sky) and non-eternal things (pot). The reason does not conclusively distinguish eternal from non-eternal.

The inference 'sound is eternal because it is produced' is Viruddha. The hetu 'produced' actually establishes the opposite of what is intended — produced things are impermanent, so the reason contradicts the sadhya (eternality).
